original_abstract: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) are major global pathogens that significantly compromise the health, longevity, and overall well-being of domestic cats. This study investigated the prevalence of these retroviruses among shelter cats in Portugal to better understand their epidemiological dynamics and clinical implications. Blood samples were collected from 326 cats during routine medical screenings upon shelter admission and analyzed using immunochromatographic assays. The overall seroprevalence for at least one of the viruses was 15.3% (50/326; 95% CI: 11.6–19.7). Specifically, 9.8% of the cats tested positive for FIV (32/326), 2.1% for FeLV (7/326), and 3.4% were co-infected with both pathogens (11/326). While infection rates for FIV and co-infections were notably higher in adult cats, no significant statistical associations were observed regarding sex, breed, hair length, or geographic municipality. As the first comprehensive report on retroviral prevalence in Portuguese shelter populations, these findings underscore the urgent need for enhanced surveillance, stricter adherence to vaccination protocols, and robust management strategies to control viral transmission within the feline population.
humanized_abstract: Title: Prevalence of feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) in cats at shelters in Portugal Abstract: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) are widespread retroviruses causing significant mortality, disease and a reduction in the life expectancy of domestic cats worldwide. This study evaluates the prevalence of FIV and FeLV retroviruses in a sample of domestic cats from shelters located in Portugal and also evaluates the clinical importance associated with their infection. Blood samples were obtained from 326 domestic cats after clinical examination at shelters. Using immunochromatographic assays, we identified the global seroprevalence of FIV and FeLV and determined the association between feline retrovirus infection and cat’s clinical characteristics. Seroprevalence for at least one of the two viruses was of 15.3% (50/326; 95% CI: 11.6–19.7). Of 326 cats, 9.8% (32/326) tested positive for FIV; 2.1% (7/326) for FeLV and 3.4% (11/326) were co-infected by both viruses. Although FIV and co-infections were confirmed in a higher number of adult cats, no significant relationship was confirmed in respect to the other studied factors (sex, breed, hair length and municipality). Based on the absence of previous studies on the presence of retroviruses in shelters in Portugal, our work strongly suggests that continuous surveillance, adherence to veterinary vaccination guidelines and efficient management of the viruses in the domestic cat population is mandatory.
